title: Repairing a delaminating concrete floor screed
subtitle: Cut out and recast a section of screed that has separated from the slab
excerpt: How to identify and repair a section of floor screed that has delaminated from the concrete slab beneath it, covering how to map the hollow area, saw-cut the perimeter, break out the failed screed, treat the slab, and lay a new patch of sand-and-cement screed to match the surrounding level.
sourceNotes: BS 8204-1: Screeds, bases and in-situ floorings. BRE Good Repair Guide 11 (floor screed defects). City and Guilds Level 2 Plastering. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: The separation of a floor screed from the structural concrete slab beneath it, creating a hollow void between the two layers. Usually caused by insufficient bond between screed and slab (no SBR bonding agent, over-wet mix that trapped laitance on the slab surface), or by moisture movement from below. Delaminated screed sounds hollow when tapped and may crack along the edges of the hollow area.

A styrene-butadiene rubber emulsion mixed with water and brushed onto a concrete substrate before applying a repair mortar or screed. Forms a tackifying layer that significantly improves the bond between old and new concrete. Mixed with cement powder to form a slurry for optimal adhesion on large areas.

content.text: screed flexes under foot traffic, cracks along the hollow boundary, and eventually collapses. It is identified by a dull resonant sound when tapped with a knuckle or a coin, like tapping on a drum rather than a flat solid surface. The repair requires cutting back to sound screed on all sides, not just filling the crack; any unsupported area of the delaminated screed will fail again at the same boundary.

Sharp sand and Portland cement for the new screed mix (3:1 or 4:1 sand to cement depending on the required strength). SBR bonding agent. PVA adhesive (for the initial slab seal). Rapid-setting screed repair mortar is an alternative for small areas (pre-mixed bags, just add water). A straight metal screed rail or a timber batten to level the patch surface.

Tap systematically across the floor in a grid pattern and mark all hollow-sounding areas with chalk or pencil. Extend the investigation beyond the visible crack or surface damage; hollow areas are consistently larger than they appear. Mark a cut line at least 50 mm into sound screed on all sides of the hollow zone.
Step 2, saw-cut the perimeter and break out the old screed

Fit a diamond blade to the angle grinder and saw-cut along the marked perimeter to the full depth of the screed (typically 50 to 75 mm). The saw-cut creates a clean vertical edge for the new screed to butt against; without it, the repair edge will be ragged and liable to further cracking. Break out the delaminated screed with the SDS drill chisel and remove all debris. Vacuum the slab surface clean.
Step 3, treat the slab and apply bonding agent
Check the exposed slab for any contamination (oil, grease, old adhesive residue). Clean with a stiff brush. Brush the

diluted 1:1 with water onto the slab surface and up the saw-cut walls. Allow to become tacky (typically 20 to 30 minutes). Mix a slurry of SBR and cement powder (50/50 by volume) and brush it over the slab just before the screed goes down; this cement-SBR slurry provides additional grip between the slab and the new screed.
Step 4, lay and level the new screed

Mix the screed at 3:1 sharp sand to cement; it should be stiff enough to hold a footprint when stepped on. Fill the prepared area in one pass. Use a screed rail or a long timber batten spanning the sound screed on each side to rule the surface level; drag the rule across the top of the sound screed to bring the new patch flush. Tamp the surface to consolidate, then rule again. Apply a steel trowel finish if the area will be tiled; a float finish if it will be carpeted.

Protect the fresh screed from foot traffic for 24 hours and from floor covering installation for at least 7 days (28 days minimum for tiles over a new screed). Cover with polythene sheeting in warm weather to prevent rapid drying, which weakens the surface. If the underlying cause of the original delamination (rising damp, poorly prepared slab surface) is not resolved, the repair will fail again.

attrs.symptom: New patch is lower than the surrounding floor by 2 to 4 mm
attrs.cause: The screed mix was too wet and slumped slightly as it cured.
attrs.fix: Apply a self-levelling floor compound over the patch once the screed has cured (at least 3 days). Self-levelling compound finds its own level and can fill low spots up to 10 mm.

attrs.symptom: Crack appears at the junction between new and old screed within a few weeks
attrs.cause: The saw-cut edge was not clean or the new screed was not pressed firmly against the existing screed at the perimeter.
attrs.fix: Rake out the crack, prime with diluted SBR, and fill with a flexible floor repair compound. This type of movement joint crack at a patch edge is common and largely cosmetic where the screed is otherwise well bonded.
